local_names.romanized_local: Ubi kayu mentah dikupas (Malay); Singkong mentah dikupas (Indonesian)

region_of_origin: South America (widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw peeled tapioca is approximately 100g, equivalent to a medium-sized chunk before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca (Cassava), raw, peeled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for tapioca raw peeled (cr_id F14070015). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cassava raw (FDC ID 169985) as 0.07g/100g. Vitamin C value of 20.6mg/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169985) as HPB does not report vitamin C for this entry. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in HPB; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cassava (tapioca root) boiled GI reported as approximately 46–94 across multiple studies in the University of Sydney GI Database; raw cassava GI is estimated at ~72 (mean of published boiled values as raw is not consumed directly). Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database list cassava boiled GI values ranging from 46 to 94 with a commonly cited central estimate of ~72. GL per serving calculated using net available carbohydrate (carbohydrate minus fibre: 34.1 − 4.6 = 29.5g; GL = 72 × 29.5 / 100 = 21.2, conservatively reported as 22.6 using total carbohydrate basis). Note: raw cassava must be cooked before consumption; GI applies to the cooked form.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (250mg/100g) provide a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Raw peeled tapioca is suitable for hypertension management due to negligible sodium and moderate potassium; cook without added salt and control portion size to manage the carbohydrate load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~72) combined with a substantial carbohydrate load of 34.1g/100g poses significant postprandial blood glucose risk despite 4.6g/100g dietary fibre.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit tapioca to small portions (50–75g cooked), pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycaemic response, and prefer boiled or cooled preparations which increase resistant starch content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.07g/100g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (4.6g/100g) support a heart-healthy nutritional profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw peeled tapioca is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it acceptable for cardiovascular disease management when consumed in moderate portions and prepared by boiling or steaming without added fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole-food source with dietary fibre and vitamin C (~20.6mg/100g) provides anti-inflammatory benefit, but must be thoroughly cooked to eliminate cyanogenic glycosides; deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Prefer boiled or steamed tapioca for rheumatoid arthritis; avoid deep-frying, and do not add coconut milk or lard which increase saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tapioca (raw, peeled) refers to the starchy tuberous root of the cassava plant (Manihot esculenta), with the outer brown skin removed to reveal the firm white flesh. It is a major staple carbohydrate crop across Southeast Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean. In Singapore and Malaysia it is known as ubi kayu; in Indonesia as singkong; in South America as yuca or manioc. The raw peeled root has a firm, dense texture and a mild, slightly sweet, earthy flavour. It must always be cooked before consumption — boiling, steaming, or baking — to neutralise naturally occurring cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin and lotaustrasin) that are toxic when raw. Per 100g, raw peeled tapioca provides approximately 154 kcal, 34.1g carbohydrate, 4.6g dietary fibre, 0.6g protein, and 0.3g fat, with negligible sodium (2mg) and meaningful potassium (250mg). Its glycaemic index is high (~72), making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dietary fibre content and very low sodium make it suitable for hypertension management under the DASH framework. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Cooling cooked cassava increases resistant starch content, modestly lowering its glycaemic impact. It is used in a wide range of preparations including boiled cassava, cassava chips, cassava cake (kuih), and as a base for tapioca pearls used in bubble tea and desserts.
common_user_queries: Is raw cassava safe to eat?
What is the GI of tapioca?
How many calories in raw cassava?
Is cassava good for high blood pressure?
What is the difference between tapioca and cassava?
meal_context: steamed and served with sambal or coconut
used in kuih and traditional cakes
base for tapioca pearls in bubble tea
breakfast staple in Southeast Asia and Africa

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14070015, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169985) for cassava raw. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for raw peeled tapioca are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14070015) and are not available in a directly matching entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. Vitamin C value of 20.6mg/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169985, cassava raw) as HPB does not report vitamin C for this entry. Saturated fat of 0.07g/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169985). Cholesterol is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0mg/100g). Raw cassava must always be cooked before consumption to eliminate cyanogenic glycosides. GI of ~72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled cassava values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; raw cassava is not consumed directly and GI applies to the cooked form. GL per serving calculated using net available carbohydrate (29.5g) x GI / 100 = 21.2, conservatively reported as 22.6 using total carbohydrate basis. Tapioca pearls (processed starch product) are a distinct food item and should not be confused with this raw root entry.
